mission: Create the communications vendor-selection dossier for telephony / IVR, SMS, and email while freezing the internal mock-now lane, the current official vendor evidence set, and the fail-closed actual-provider-later shortlist used by seq_032 and seq_033.

recommended_strategy.label: Split-vendor posture preferred
recommended_strategy.summary: Keep telephony and notifications independently selectable. Do not shortlist any combined suite as the default because family-specific truth, fallback, and callback semantics outweigh procurement simplicity.

lane_matrix.handoff_focus: workspace, number, answer/event/fallback webhooks, recording, and spend gates
lane_matrix.notes: Selected mock-now telephony twin. Must preserve call session lifecycle, IVR, recording presence vs absence, transcript readiness, urgent-live preemption, webhook retries, and continuation handoff.

lane_matrix.handoff_focus: project, sender, webhook, continuation routing, and spend gates
lane_matrix.notes: Selected mock-now SMS rail. Must preserve accepted vs delayed vs bounced vs expired delivery, wrong-recipient risk, replay-safe callbacks, and non-authoritative transport acceptance.

lane_matrix.handoff_focus: project or subuser, sender/domain auth, event webhooks, and spend gates
lane_matrix.notes: Selected mock-now email rail. Must preserve accepted vs delivered vs delayed vs bounced vs complained vs disputed outcomes and controlled resend under the same authoritative chain.

lane_matrix.handoff_focus: combined-suite posture only; telephony and notification tasks stay separately gated
lane_matrix.notes: Mock-now execution stays on the internal combined signal fabric even though real providers are researched now. No external vendor is allowed to become lifecycle authority during MVP buildout.

telephony_ivr.notes: Selected mock-now telephony twin. Must preserve call session lifecycle, IVR, recording presence vs absence, transcript readiness, urgent-live preemption, webhook retries, and continuation handoff.

sms.notes: Selected mock-now SMS rail. Must preserve accepted vs delayed vs bounced vs expired delivery, wrong-recipient risk, replay-safe callbacks, and non-authoritative transport acceptance.

email.notes: Selected mock-now email rail. Must preserve accepted vs delivered vs delayed vs bounced vs complained vs disputed outcomes and controlled resend under the same authoritative chain.

combined.notes: Mock-now execution stays on the internal combined signal fabric even though real providers are researched now. No external vendor is allowed to become lifecycle authority during MVP buildout.

telephony_ivr.top_note: Strong public sandbox and recording support, but callback authenticity relies on signature validation plus adapter-side dedupe rather than a first-class replay token.
telephony_ivr.handoff_focus: workspace, number, answer/event/fallback webhooks, recording, and spend gates

telephony_ivr.top_note: Vonage scores well on signed callbacks, fallback webhooks, DTMF, and recording URLs. It remains slightly behind Twilio on public test-environment depth.
telephony_ivr.handoff_focus: workspace, number, answer/event/fallback webhooks, recording, and spend gates

sms.top_note: High-quality API and callback coverage, but no first-class replay token in the reviewed webhook docs means the adapter must own replay defense.
sms.handoff_focus: project, sender, webhook, continuation routing, and spend gates

sms.top_note: The strongest current SMS-specific callback security story in the reviewed public material; remains shortlisted despite lighter sandbox depth than Twilio.
sms.handoff_focus: project, sender, webhook, continuation routing, and spend gates

email.top_note: Best fit for authoritative delivery ambiguity, explicit replay defense, EU region control, and project-style subaccount segmentation.
email.handoff_focus: project or subuser, sender/domain auth, event webhooks, and spend gates

email.top_note: Strong webhook security and EU subuser posture offset the sandbox limitation that no event webhooks fire during sandbox-mode validation.
email.handoff_focus: project or subuser, sender/domain auth, event webhooks, and spend gates

telephony_ivr.notes: Technically capable enough to remain in the universe, but the current public evidence set is thinner on sandbox and commercial operations than Twilio or Vonage.

sms.notes: Sinch remains a credible universe row because its delivery-report callbacks are detailed, but the public callback-security story is weaker for the exact SMS surface.

combined.notes: Interesting medium-term portfolio option, but the reviewed public evidence still favors split vendors for lower coupling and clearer sandbox-to-live behavior.
A credible combined-suite candidate only. It is not shortlisted because split-vendor posture reduces coupling and avoids one vendor becoming hidden lifecycle authority.

email.kill_switch_reason_if_any: Rejected because the reviewed official webhook guidance exposes custom headers and basic auth but no signed or replay-safe callback proof, which fails the seq_031 delivery-evidence bar.

combined.kill_switch_reason_if_any: Rejected because there is no reviewed first-party Vonage email rail to satisfy the all-three combined strategy.

seq_032.focus: telephony account, workspace, number, webhook, recording, and spend controls

seq_033.focus: SMS and email project, sender/domain, webhook, and spend controls
